stem: You are the gynaecology registrar seeing a 33-year-old non-Aboriginal woman in the early pregnancy assessment clinic after three consecutive first-trimester miscarriages. Each was confirmed as intrauterine non-viable on ultrasound between 8 and 10 weeks gestation. She has no living children, no history of stillbirth, and her partner is the same throughout. She is otherwise well, with a BMI of 24 kg/m2 and a regular 28-day cycle. There is no family history of thrombosis or autoimmune disease.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/72 mmHg, HR 72, RR 14, T 36.7 °C, BMI 24 kg/m2
data_table.Examination: Unremarkable systemic examination, normal pelvic examination with a normal-sized anteverted uterus
data_table.Investigations: None ordered prior to this visit

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Reassure that three miscarriages is within statistical noise and arrange routine antenatal care at the next positive pregnancy test without specific investigations
options.rationale: Recurrent miscarriage (3 or more consecutive losses in Australia) increases the chance of finding a treatable cause to around 50%; reassurance alone misses antiphospholipid syndrome which is treatable. (Source: RANZCOG Recurrent Miscarriage Statement)

options.text: Arrange parental karyotyping, antiphospholipid antibody screen including lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin and anti-beta2 glycoprotein I, plus pelvic ultrasound
options.rationale: Three consecutive first-trimester miscarriages meet RANZCOG criteria for recurrent miscarriage workup including parental karyotype, antiphospholipid antibodies and uterine assessment. (Source: RANZCOG Recurrent Miscarriage Statement)

options.text: Order an inherited thrombophilia panel including factor V Leiden, prothrombin gene mutation and protein C and S, and commence empirical aspirin 100 mg daily
options.rationale: Inherited thrombophilia screening is not routinely recommended for first-trimester recurrent miscarriage; the evidence base does not support empirical aspirin without a positive antiphospholipid result. (Source: RANZCOG Recurrent Miscarriage Statement)

options.text: Refer for in-vitro fertilisation with preimplantation genetic testing for aneuploidy on the basis that her losses are likely from foetal chromosomal abnormality
options.rationale: Preimplantation genetic testing is not first-line for recurrent miscarriage; investigation precedes assisted reproduction decisions and there is no evidence of subfertility. (Source: RANZCOG Recurrent Miscarriage Statement)

options.text: Arrange a hysteroscopy with endometrial biopsy and screen for chronic endometritis with CD138 immunohistochemistry as initial recurrent miscarriage workup measures
options.rationale: Hysteroscopy is reserved for abnormal pelvic ultrasound or suspected uterine cavity pathology; endometritis screening is not routine first-line testing. (Source: RANZCOG Recurrent Miscarriage Statement)

explanation.summary: Recurrent miscarriage is defined in Australia as 3 or more consecutive pregnancy losses before 20 weeks. Investigation identifies a treatable cause in around half of couples. Standard first-line workup includes parental karyotyping, antiphospholipid antibody screen, pelvic ultrasound, and thyroid function. Antiphospholipid syndrome is treated in subsequent pregnancies with aspirin plus low-molecular-weight heparin. Inherited thrombophilia screening is not routinely indicated for first-trimester losses.
key_points: Recurrent miscarriage in Australia is 3 or more consecutive losses before 20 weeks
Investigation finds a treatable cause in about 50% of couples
Parental karyotype, antiphospholipid antibodies and pelvic ultrasound are first-line
Antiphospholipid syndrome warrants aspirin plus LMWH in subsequent pregnancies
Inherited thrombophilia panels are not routinely indicated for early pregnancy losses
explanation.pearls: Antiphospholipid antibody testing must be repeated 12 weeks apart with persistent positivity required for syndrome diagnosis; a single positive test is not sufficient and treatment based on one result is overtreatment.

stem: You are the obstetric medicine registrar in the joint diabetes-antenatal clinic seeing a 28-year-old non-Aboriginal woman with type 1 diabetes for 15 years who is planning her first pregnancy in the next 6 months. She uses multiple daily injections of insulin glargine and aspart and self-monitors her capillary glucose 6 times daily. She has background retinopathy and stable microalbuminuria. She takes no other medications and is not currently using contraception consistently.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 124/76 mmHg, HR 74, RR 14, T 36.6 °C, BMI 25 kg/m2
data_table.Examination: Normal cardiovascular examination, fundi show background retinopathy without proliferative changes documented last month
data_table.Investigations: HbA1c 8.2% (66 mmol/mol), urine albumin:creatinine ratio 12 mg/mmol (mild), eGFR 92 mL/min/1.73m2, TSH 1.8 mIU/L, recent retinal review documented

lead_in: What is the single best initial intervention?

options.text: Defer conception, optimise glycaemia targeting HbA1c under 6.5% with intensified insulin regimen, commence high-dose folic acid 5 mg daily, and continue effective contraception
options.rationale: ADIPS preconception care for type 1 diabetes targets HbA1c under 6.5% to reduce congenital anomaly risk, high-dose folate from preconception, and effective contraception until glycaemic targets are reached. (Source: ADIPS Diabetes in Pregnancy Guideline)

options.text: Encourage immediate conception attempts, switch from insulin glargine to detemir as the preferred basal insulin in pregnancy, and arrange dating ultrasound at confirmation of pregnancy
options.rationale: Conception with HbA1c over 8% carries a 4 to 10 fold increased risk of major congenital malformation; optimisation is needed first. Glargine has acceptable pregnancy safety data and a switch is not required. (Source: ADIPS Diabetes in Pregnancy Guideline)

options.text: Commence metformin 500 mg twice daily in addition to insulin to improve glycaemic control, arrange repeat HbA1c in 6 weeks, and start folic acid 500 micrograms daily
options.rationale: Metformin is not the standard adjunct in type 1 diabetes preconception and folic acid dose should be 5 mg (not 500 micrograms) due to higher neural tube defect baseline risk. (Source: ADIPS Diabetes in Pregnancy Guideline)

options.text: Refer to ophthalmology for prophylactic pan-retinal photocoagulation given the background retinopathy and high HbA1c, and defer all conception planning until that procedure has been completed
options.rationale: Photocoagulation is for proliferative or severe non-proliferative disease, not background retinopathy; pregnancy planning continues alongside ophthalmology surveillance. (Source: ADIPS Diabetes in Pregnancy Guideline)

options.text: Refer to nephrology before conception for renal biopsy of the microalbuminuria, withhold pregnancy planning until biopsy is completed, and continue current insulin regimen and contraception
options.rationale: Microalbuminuria with preserved eGFR in established type 1 diabetes is expected and does not require biopsy; nephrology referral is reserved for declining GFR or heavy proteinuria. (Source: ADIPS Diabetes in Pregnancy Guideline)

explanation.summary: Preconception care in type 1 diabetes aims for HbA1c under 6.5%, folate 5 mg daily from at least 3 months pre-pregnancy, effective contraception until targets are reached, retinal review and renal assessment. ACE inhibitors, statins and ARBs are stopped before conception. CGM access during pregnancy improves outcomes and is funded for type 1 diabetes in Australia. Folic acid 5 mg compensates for higher neural tube defect risk.
key_points: Target HbA1c under 6.5% before conception in type 1 diabetes
Folic acid 5 mg daily from preconception through first trimester
Stop ACE inhibitors, ARBs and statins before conception
Document retinal and renal status before pregnancy
Continuous glucose monitoring is funded for type 1 diabetes in pregnancy in Australia
explanation.pearls: Insulin requirements typically fall in the first trimester (risk of hypoglycaemia), then rise from 18 weeks to peak around 36 weeks at 1.5 to 2 times pre-pregnancy doses; women should be counselled to anticipate these shifts before pregnancy.

subtopic_detail: Monochorionic diamniotic twin surveillance for TTTS
stem: You are the maternal-foetal medicine fellow reviewing a 34-year-old non-Aboriginal woman with a monochorionic diamniotic twin pregnancy at 19+3 weeks. She has been having fortnightly scans. At her current scan there is a discordance of estimated foetal weight with one twin at the 18th centile and the other at the 62nd. Amniotic fluid deepest vertical pocket is 2.1 cm around twin A and 9.5 cm around twin B. Both bladders are visible.

data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender uterus, no contractions, foetal hearts both present
data_table.Investigations: Ultrasound DVP twin A 2.1 cm, twin B 9.5 cm, EFW twin A 18th centile, twin B 62nd centile, both bladders visible, no hydrops, cervical length 35 mm, Doppler middle cerebral artery and umbilical artery within normal range

lead_in: What is the single best next step?

options.text: Administer betamethasone for foetal lung maturation, arrange caesarean section in the next 24 hours, and admit to the antenatal ward pending neonatal team handover
options.rationale: Delivery at 19 weeks is below viability and would be lethal; medical therapy is foetoscopic laser. (Source: RANZCOG Multiple Pregnancy Statement)

options.text: Continue routine fortnightly surveillance, reassure that mild fluid discordance is common in monochorionic twins, and review with repeat scan in 2 weeks for trend assessment
options.rationale: DVP discordance crossing the 2 and 8 cm thresholds defines TTTS and is not normal variation; delay risks rapid progression to Stage II with disappearing bladder. (Source: RANZCOG Multiple Pregnancy Statement)

options.text: Arrange immediate amnioreduction of the polyhydramniotic sac by removing 1500 mL of amniotic fluid to relieve pressure, with subsequent weekly scan follow-up
options.rationale: Amnioreduction is now second-line in TTTS and is reserved for circumstances where fetoscopic laser is unavailable or contraindicated; laser is the definitive Australian first-line treatment for Stage II and above and most Stage I cases. (Source: RANZCOG Multiple Pregnancy Statement)

options.text: Arrange urgent referral to the foetal therapy service for assessment for fetoscopic laser ablation of placental anastomoses within the next week
options.rationale: DVP under 2 cm in one sac plus over 8 cm in the other meets Quintero stage I twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome; in monochorionic twins this mandates referral to a foetal therapy centre because Stage II to IV may develop quickly. (Source: RANZCOG Multiple Pregnancy Statement)

options.text: Diagnose selective foetal growth restriction without TTTS, arrange weekly Doppler surveillance, and continue conservative management until 36 weeks with planned caesarean section
options.rationale: The DVP discordance and absence of severe Doppler abnormalities fits TTTS more than isolated selective growth restriction; both can coexist but the immediate referral pathway is the same. (Source: RANZCOG Multiple Pregnancy Statement)

explanation.summary: Twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome complicates around 10% of monochorionic diamniotic pregnancies via unbalanced placental vascular anastomoses. Quintero staging uses bladder, Doppler and hydrops findings; Stage I is amniotic fluid discordance with both bladders visible. Australian management funnels through a foetal therapy centre, where fetoscopic laser ablation between 16 and 26 weeks is the definitive first-line treatment for Stage II and beyond and most Stage I cases. Surveillance in MCDA twins is fortnightly from 16 weeks.
key_points: TTTS occurs in around 10% of monochorionic diamniotic twins
Quintero Stage I is DVP under 2 cm in one sac and over 8 cm in the other with both bladders visible
Fortnightly ultrasound surveillance from 16 weeks is standard for MCDA twins
Fetoscopic laser ablation is the first-line treatment in Australia for Stage II and above and most Stage I
Amnioreduction is second-line when laser is unavailable
explanation.pearls: Recipient twin features include polyhydramnios, large bladder and cardiac hypertrophy from volume overload; donor twin features include oligohydramnios, small or absent bladder (stuck twin) and growth restriction, and the mortality rate without treatment exceeds 80% at Stage III.

subtopic_detail: Painless antepartum haemorrhage at 32 weeks with low-lying placenta
stem: You are the obstetrics registrar called to triage for a 32-year-old non-Aboriginal woman in her second pregnancy at 32+4 weeks with sudden painless bright-red vaginal bleeding of around 150 mL, no contractions and good foetal movements. Her morphology scan reported a posterior placenta extending to 1 cm from the internal cervical os. She has had one previous caesarean section. The CTG is reassuring. She is haemodynamically stable.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 92, RR 18, SpO2 99% room air, T 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender uterus, foetus cephalic, foetal heart 142 bpm reactive, no palpable contractions, no abdominal tenderness, no signs of shock, perineum stained with bright-red blood approximately 150 mL on the pad
data_table.Investigations: FBC Hb 112 g/L platelets 215 x10^9/L, group and hold sent, fibrinogen 4.2 g/L, CTG reassuring, bedside ultrasound confirms posterior low-lying placenta within 1 cm of internal os and foetus appropriately grown

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence intravenous magnesium sulfate for tocolysis and foetal neuroprotection, administer methyldopa for the elevated maternal pulse, and review obstetric Doppler studies in 24 hours
options.rationale: There is no contraction or hypertension to treat; magnesium for tocolysis is not standard and methyldopa is not indicated. The maternal tachycardia reflects anxiety and mild blood loss, not hypertension. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia and Accreta Statement)
source_refs: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia and Accreta Statement

options.text: Perform a gentle digital vaginal examination to assess cervical dilatation, send a high vaginal swab, and continue ward monitoring with CTG every 4 hours for the next 24 hours
options.rationale: Digital examination in low-lying placenta can precipitate catastrophic haemorrhage and is contraindicated until placental position is confirmed not to be praevia. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia and Accreta Statement)
source_refs: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia and Accreta Statement

options.text: Arrange immediate category 1 caesarean section under general anaesthesia within 30 minutes given the antepartum haemorrhage and prior caesarean section history
options.rationale: Category 1 caesarean is reserved for maternal or foetal compromise; this woman is stable with reassuring CTG and the priority is corticosteroids and stabilisation. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia and Accreta Statement)
source_refs: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia and Accreta Statement

options.text: Discharge home with follow-up scan in 2 weeks, telephone parental education for any recurrent bleed and routine community midwife review at the next antenatal visit in a fortnight
options.rationale: Active bleeding with low-lying placenta requires inpatient observation due to the high risk of recurrent and unpredictable major haemorrhage. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia and Accreta Statement)
source_refs: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia and Accreta Statement

options.text: Admit to the antenatal ward, administer betamethasone 11.4 mg IM and book Anti-D, withhold digital and speculum examination, and arrange transfer to a centre with neonatal intensive care capability
options.rationale: Bleeding with low-lying placenta within 2 cm of os requires inpatient observation, corticosteroids before 34+6 weeks, Anti-D for Rh-negative or to confirm need, no digital exam (haemorrhage risk), and tertiary transfer for neonatal capability. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia and Accreta Statement)
source_refs: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia and Accreta Statement

explanation.summary: Painless antepartum bleeding with a placenta within 2 cm of the internal os should be managed as placenta praevia until proven otherwise. Australian management is inpatient observation, antenatal corticosteroids before 34+6 weeks, Rh status check with Anti-D for Rh-negative women, avoidance of digital examination, and tertiary transfer for neonatal capability. Planned caesarean is at 36 to 37+6 weeks for major praevia. Prior caesarean section plus anterior low-lying placenta should also trigger evaluation for placenta accreta spectrum.
key_points: Painless APH with placenta within 2 cm of os is praevia until proven otherwise
No digital examination until praevia is excluded
Antenatal corticosteroids if before 34+6 weeks
Tertiary transfer for neonatal intensive care capability
Prior caesarean plus anterior low-lying placenta requires placenta accreta spectrum evaluation
explanation.pearls: A Kleihauer-Betke test should be sent in any Rh-negative woman with significant antepartum bleeding to size the foeto-maternal haemorrhage and dose Anti-D 625 IU per 6 mL of foetal red cell volume in excess of standard dose.

sources.label: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia and Accreta Statement

subtopic_detail: Intravenous iron in third-trimester iron deficiency anaemia
stem: You are the antenatal clinic registrar reviewing a 30-year-old non-Aboriginal G3P2 woman at 32+0 weeks gestation with fatigue and pallor on routine screening. She has been taking oral ferrous sulfate 325 mg daily since 18 weeks but stopped 4 weeks ago because of nausea and constipation. She has no haemoglobinopathy and her booking ferritin was 14 microg/L. There is no clinical bleeding and no plan for elective caesarean section. She is keen to be well before her induction date booked at 39+0 weeks.

data_table.Examination: Pale conjunctivae, no jaundice, soft uterus appropriate for gestation, foetal heart 144 bpm
data_table.Investigations: Hb 92 g/L, MCV 76 fL, ferritin 8 microg/L (deficient under 30 in pregnancy), transferrin saturation 8%, B12 and folate normal, CRP 4 mg/L, urinalysis nil

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Recommence oral ferrous sulfate 325 mg three times daily with vitamin C and review the FBC in 4 weeks, with no plan for IV iron unless oral therapy fails at the next review
options.rationale: Higher-dose oral iron worsens gastrointestinal side effects and rarely reaches target Hb in this timeframe; she has documented intolerance and only 7 weeks to birth. (Source: RANZCOG Iron Deficiency Anaemia in Pregnancy Statement)
source_refs: RANZCOG Iron Deficiency Anaemia in Pregnancy Statement

options.text: Arrange intravenous ferric carboxymaltose infusion 1000 mg in day stay, recheck the FBC and ferritin in 4 weeks, and counsel that oral iron can be re-trialled postpartum if needed
options.rationale: Iron deficiency anaemia at 32 weeks with prior intolerance and short interval to birth (under 8 weeks) is an indication for IV iron in Australia; ferric carboxymaltose 1000 mg is the standard single-dose option and replenishes stores rapidly before delivery. (Source: RANZCOG Iron Deficiency Anaemia in Pregnancy Statement)
source_refs: RANZCOG Iron Deficiency Anaemia in Pregnancy Statement
Australian Red Cross Lifeblood Iron Guidance

options.text: Transfuse 1 unit of packed red cells given the symptomatic anaemia and short time to delivery, and counsel that iron stores will be replenished by the transfusion
options.rationale: Transfusion is reserved for severe anaemia (typically Hb under 70 g/L) or symptomatic compromise; ferric carboxymaltose is preferred and avoids transfusion risks while replenishing stores. (Source: Australian Red Cross Lifeblood Iron Guidance)
source_refs: Australian Red Cross Lifeblood Iron Guidance

options.text: Switch oral iron to ferrous fumarate 200 mg every second day with the rationale of reduced hepcidin response, and schedule routine community midwife review in 2 weeks for FBC check
options.rationale: Alternate-day oral dosing improves absorption efficiency but the absolute uptake is still small and insufficient before delivery with this Hb and timeframe. (Source: RANZCOG Iron Deficiency Anaemia in Pregnancy Statement)
source_refs: RANZCOG Iron Deficiency Anaemia in Pregnancy Statement

options.text: Arrange erythropoiesis-stimulating agent darbepoetin 100 micrograms weekly given the poor response to oral iron and the short timeframe before her planned induction at 39 weeks
options.rationale: Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents are not standard for pregnancy iron deficiency anaemia in the absence of chronic kidney disease; IV iron alone is the recommended escalation. (Source: RANZCOG Iron Deficiency Anaemia in Pregnancy Statement)
source_refs: RANZCOG Iron Deficiency Anaemia in Pregnancy Statement

explanation.summary: Australian guidance treats iron deficiency anaemia in pregnancy with oral iron as first-line, switching to intravenous iron if there is intolerance, malabsorption, severe anaemia or insufficient time to delivery. Ferric carboxymaltose 1000 mg as a single infusion is the preferred IV product, given from the second trimester. Replenishing iron before birth reduces postpartum transfusion and improves maternal symptoms. Postpartum iron deficiency is also common and warrants screening at 6 weeks.
key_points: Treat IDA in pregnancy with oral iron first-line
Switch to IV iron for intolerance, malabsorption, severe anaemia or short time to delivery
Ferric carboxymaltose 1000 mg single infusion is standard in Australia from second trimester
Transfusion is reserved for severe anaemia or compromise, generally Hb under 70 g/L
Postpartum iron deficiency screening at 6 weeks identifies persistent depletion
explanation.pearls: Alternate-day oral iron lowers hepcidin response and yields slightly higher fractional absorption than daily dosing; however, in pregnancy the absolute dose required to correct deficiency by term is rarely achievable by oral route alone once Hb is under 100 g/L past 30 weeks.

sources.label: RANZCOG Iron Deficiency Anaemia in Pregnancy Statement
Australian Red Cross Lifeblood Iron Guidance
